nativeRange.habitat: Rocky shores and sand beds in shallow tropical waters; commonly found under basalt rocks at the high-tide level and on adjacent sand patches where it forages at night.

diet.notes: Has been documented as a small predatory scavenger — feeds on detritus, uneaten meaty foods, decomposing organisms, polychaete worms, and small sand-dwelling invertebrates. Will accept supplemental chopped mysis or brine shrimp when natural food is scarce. Does NOT eat algae meaningfully; treat as a sand-bed/rockwork carnivore-detritivore rather than an algae grazer.

avoidWith: small or juvenile snails (may be predated)
feather dusters and small tube worms (may be predated)

summary: A tiny black-and-yellow striped predatory snail commonly added to saltwater clean-up crews. Generally considered coral-safe but is a small carnivore that has been reported to predate vermetid snails, polychaetes, and occasionally other small snails.
careNotes: Bumblebee Snail (Engina mendicaria, also formerly placed in the genus Pusiostoma) is a small Indo-Pacific buccinoid snail in the family Pisaniidae (some retailers still list it under the older family Buccinidae, and LiveAquaria currently misattributes it to Trochidae). Adults reach roughly 1.5-2.2 cm shell length; sources disagree, with LiveAquaria stating up to 3.8 cm and most other care guides citing 1-2 cm — averaged toward the lower-end consensus. Generally advised to keep around 22-26 C, pH 8.1-8.4, and SG 1.023-1.025. The species is primarily nocturnal and tends to spend daylight hours buried in the sand bed or wedged into rockwork crevices. IMPORTANT — common-name collision: the freshwater 'Bumblebee Snail' (also called Assassin Snail) is Anentome helena / Clea helena (fw-moll-007), a totally different species; double-check which one is intended when buying. Despite the gentle name, Bumblebee Snails are small predators, not algae grazers: they have been reported to consume vermetid snails (popular among reef-keepers wanting natural vermetid control, though they are slow and rarely handle heavy infestations), polychaete worms, and — if underfed — smaller snails in the cleanup crew. They are coral-safe, but 'reef-safe' should be qualified: a hungry Bumblebee Snail can harass or predate other beneficial inverts. Copper-sensitive like all marine inverts; do not expose to copper-based medications. Source spread on adult size: 1.3 cm (one care site, likely a unit-conversion error), 1.5-2.2 cm (AquariumBreeder), 3.8 cm (LiveAquaria) — averaged toward 1.5-2.2 cm as the modal value.
breedingNotes: Has been reported as extremely difficult to breed in the home aquarium. There are no externally visible sexually dimorphic features, making pair selection essentially guesswork, and larval-rearing protocols for this species are not documented in the hobby literature.

additional.name: Bulk Reef Supply - Handling Vermetid Snails
additional.notes: Secondary source per Stage A manifest; BRS has no dedicated product page for Engina mendicaria but content posts confirm vermetid-predation role and slow effectiveness against heavy infestations.

additional.name: AquariumBreeder - Bumble Bee Snail Detailed Guide
additional.notes: Third source for size (1.5-2.2 cm), temperature (22-25 C), pH (8.1-8.4), salinity (SG 1.023-1.025), and 20 L minimum tank. Cited family as Buccinidae (older classification).

additional.name: Wikipedia - Engina mendicaria
additional.notes: Confirmed modern taxonomy (Family Pisaniidae, Order Neogastropoda), authorship (Linnaeus, 1758), native range across Red Sea and Indo-Pacific, shell size 10-20 mm, and nocturnal carnivore lifestyle.

additional.name: SaltwaterAquariumBlog - Bumblebee Snail Care Guide
additional.notes: Fourth source confirming carnivore diet, predation on smaller snails when underfed, ~2 year lifespan, and 19 L (5 gal) minimum tank per snail.
